topics.title: Arrays & Hashing
topics.description: Master the fundamentals: traversal, two-pointer, and hash-map patterns for O(n) solutions.

lessons.title: The Two-Sum Pattern
lessons.summary: Learn how a hash map reduces a brute-force O(n²) search to a single O(n) pass.

content.text:
## The Core Idea

Imagine you're at a party looking for someone whose age adds up to exactly 30 with yours. The brute-force way is to ask every person to check every other person — **O(n²)**.

The smart way: as you walk through the room, write your own age on a sticky note on your back. Now anyone who needs you can spot you instantly — **O(n)**.

That sticky-note board is your **hash map**.

content.caption: O(n) Two-Sum using a hash map

content.text:
## Step-by-Step Trace

For `nums = [2, 7, 11, 15]`, `target = 9`:

| i | num | complement | seen (before insert) | result |
|---|-----|------------|----------------------|--------|
| 0 | 2   | 7          | `{}`                 | not found, add 2 |
| 1 | 7   | 2          | `{2: 0}`             | **found!** → `[0, 1]` |

We never look at indices 2 or 3.

content.question: What is the time complexity of the hash-map Two-Sum approach?
content.explanation: We iterate through the array once. Each hash-map lookup and insertion is O(1) on average, giving us O(n) overall.

lessons.title: Sliding Window Fundamentals
lessons.summary: Replace nested loops with a single expanding/shrinking window for subarray and substring problems.

content.text:
## What Is a Sliding Window?

Think of a train window. As the train moves, the view shifts — new scenery enters on one side, old scenery leaves the other. You never step off and walk back.

In code, **two pointers** (`left` and `right`) mark the window edges. We expand `right` to grow the window and advance `left` to shrink it.

content.caption: Maximum sum subarray of size k — O(n)
content.code:
def max_subarray_sum(nums: list[int], k: int) -> int:
    window_sum = sum(nums[:k])
    max_sum = window_sum
    for i in range(k, len(nums)):
        window_sum += nums[i] - nums[i - k]  # slide: add right, drop left
        max_sum = max(max_sum, window_sum)
    return max_sum

topics.title: Dynamic Programming
topics.description: Break hard problems into overlapping subproblems. Learn memoization and tabulation techniques.

lessons.title: DP Explained via Fibonacci
lessons.summary: Understand memoization and tabulation by optimizing the classic Fibonacci sequence.

content.text:
## The Problem with Naive Recursion

The naive recursive Fibonacci calls `fib(3)`, `fib(2)`, `fib(1)` many times. For `fib(50)` this explodes to over a trillion calls.

**Dynamic Programming** says: *compute each subproblem once, store the answer.*

content.caption: Top-down memoization
Bottom-up tabulation — O(n) time, O(1) space

content.question: What is the space complexity of the bottom-up tabulated Fibonacci above?
content.explanation: We only track two variables (prev and curr), not a full array. So space is constant — O(1).
